title: Measurement-induced phase transitions in disordered fermions
authors: Yunxiang Liao, Max Matheussen, Xinghai Zhang
abstract: Measurement-induced phase transitions are nonequilibrium transitions between phases characterized by distinct entanglement scaling behaviors, driven by the competition between unitary dynamics and measurements. Despite recent numerical efforts, how quenched disorder affects these transitions remains unclear. In this work, we study a $d$-dimensional noninteracting fermionic system subject to both quenched disorder and continuous monitoring of the local particle density, and derive an effective field theory describing its long-time universal behaviors. We find that the system is governed by the same nonlinear sigma model as in the case of clean monitored fermions, with disorder entering only through a modification of model parameters. This result suggests that the presence or absence of a measurement-induced phase transition is unaffected by the introduction of disorder: in spatial dimensions d>1, a transition occurs between an area x log law phase and an area law phase, whereas in d=1, the system exhibits only an area law phase and no transition. Numerical results further demonstrate that both clean and disordered one-dimensional free fermions exhibit area-law behavior when the system size is large enough.
summary: This paper demonstrates that quenched disorder does not alter the fundamental existence of measurement-induced phase transitions in fermionic systems. While disorder modifies the effective parameters of the system, the transition remains absent in 1D and present in higher dimensions. The study provides a unified effective field theory describing both clean and disordered monitored fermions.
main_problem: The study investigates how quenched disorder affects measurement-induced phase transitions (MIPTs) in noninteracting fermionic systems, specifically whether disorder can induce or stabilize a transition in 1D.
main_result: The authors find that disorder does not change the existence of the MIPT; a transition exists in d > 1 between an area x log law and an area law phase, but the 1D system remains strictly in an area law phase. The effective field theory (NLsigmaM) for disordered fermions is identical to the clean case, with disorder only modifying model parameters.
method: The work employs a Replica Keldysh Path Integral approach to derive an effective two-matrix nonlinear sigma model (NLsigmaM) and uses Renormalization Group (RG) analysis alongside large-scale numerical simulations of a quantum projection protocol.
model_or_system: A d-dimensional noninteracting fermionic system subject to both quenched on-site disorder and continuous monitoring of the local particle density.
key_observables: Von Neumann entanglement entropy, particle number cumulants, particle number covariance, and the average-density response function.
important_parameters: Measurement strength (kappa), disorder strength (related to elastic scattering time), spatial dimension (d), and measurement-induced vs. disorder-induced decay rates (gamma_m and gamma_D).
main_assumptions: The system is noninteracting (free fermions), the initial state is Gaussian, and the derivation assumes a continuum limit and neglects certain nonlocal-in-time massive modes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows steady-state covariance vs. measurement rate for various disorder strengths and system sizes, with crossing points indicating the phase behavior. Figure 2 tracks the drift of finite-size crossing points toward zero measurement rate to demonstrate the absence of a transition in 1D.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of MIPT in disordered systems, develops a Keldysh field theory and NLsigmaM derivation, performs RG analysis to predict phase stability, and concludes with numerical verification in 1D.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a rigorous field-theoretic proof that static disorder cannot stabilize a measurement-induced critical phase in 1D, clarifying the robustness of MIPTs.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The covariance GAB of the steady state as a function of measurement rate for various disorder strength and system size. The geometry of A and B region in the 1D chain is illus- trated in (a) and we have LA = LB = d = L/4. The stars in (b)–(f) represent the crossing point of adjacent system sizes and the color is the same as that of the larger one. The stars for larger system sizes with strong disorder are not shown as GAB already show area-law decreasing with L and no cross- ings are observed. The data is averaged over 1000 quantum trajectories for L = 32, · · · , 256; 400 quantum trajectories for L = 512; 100 quantum trajectories for L = 1024; and 50 quantum trajectories for L =...

FIG. 2. Finite size crossing point of GAB curves of adjacent system size in Fig. 1. The decreasing of the crossing point with increasing system size L indicates the absence of genuine MIPT in thermodynamic limit.
FIG. 3. The system size scaling of GAB for various mea- surement rate and disorder strength. The covariance initially increases with system size, but it rapidly reverse the trend to decrease with L.
